subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
title: Green Firecrackers
focus: Composition, certification, and types of green firecrackers (SWAS, SAFAL, STAR).
rationale: High probability for UPSC prelims due to specific chemical compositions (absence of barium, use of potassium nitrate) and acronyms (SWAS, SAFAL, STAR) that are frequently tested.
sectionExcerpt: Easing temporarily the blanket ban on firecrackers in Delhi-NCR, Supreme Court allowed use of government-approved green crackers devoid of harmful chemicals and reducing air pollution. About Green Firecrackers: Composition: Low Thermite amount, minimum usage of Aluminium. Does not contain barium substance, used in firecrackers to add green colour. Uses Potassium Nitrate as oxidant. Benefit: Reduce at least 30 % emissions using particulate matter. Identification and Certification: CSIR NEERI Logo. Types of Green Firecrackers: SWAS (Safe Water & Air Releaser): Emit very fine water droplets that absorb dust. SAFAL (Safe Minimal Aluminium): Contain safe amount of aluminium & are quieter. STAR (Safe Thermite Cracker): Free from potassium nitrate and sulfur; emit minimal smoke.
evidenceBullets: Green firecrackers do not contain barium, which is conventionally used to add green color.
They use Potassium Nitrate as an oxidant and have low thermite and minimal aluminium.
They reduce particulate matter emissions by at least 30%.
They are identified and certified by the CSIR NEERI logo.
SWAS (Safe Water & Air Releaser) emits fine water droplets to absorb dust.
STAR (Safe Thermite Cracker) is free from potassium nitrate and sulfur.

subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
title: Flue Gas Desulphurisation (FGD)
focus: Mechanism and policy regarding FGD in coal-fired power plants.
rationale: Important industrial pollution control technology. UPSC often asks about specific pollutants targeted (SO2) and the chemical reagents used in such processes.
sectionExcerpt: A study commissioned by Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser recommended rollback of 2015 policy mandating installation of FGD systems in all of India's coal-fired plants. Rather, it recommended FGD to only those plants using imported coal or high (>0.5%) sulphur coal. About FGD: Involves removal of SO2 (Sulphur Dioxide) in exhaust gases from coal-fired power plants before being released to atmosphere. Uses scrubbing technique involving alkaline reagent (typically a sodium- or calcium-based alkaline reagent). Types of FGD Systems: Dry Sorbent Injection, Wet Limestone Based, Sea Water Based, etc.
evidenceBullets: FGD involves the removal of Sulphur Dioxide (SO2) from the exhaust gases of coal-fired power plants.
It uses a scrubbing technique that typically involves a sodium- or calcium-based alkaline reagent.
A recent study recommended limiting the FGD mandate to plants using imported or high-sulphur (>0.5%) coal rather than all plants.

subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
title: Taj Trapezium Zone (TTZ)
focus: Geographical coverage, statutory backing, and regulatory framework of the TTZ.
rationale: Combines geography (heritage sites), environment (pollution control), and statutory bodies (EPA 1986), making it a classic UPSC multi-statement question candidate.
sectionExcerpt: Supreme Court directed NEERI to assess the impact of neighbouring glass industry on Taj Mahal. SC in past issued orders from time to time for TTZ. About TTZ: Location: 10,400 sq km area (shaped like trapezoid) around Taj Mahal for preventing its pollution. Coverage: Three World Heritage Sites- Taj Mahal, Agra Fort, and Fatehpur Sikri. Taj Trapezium Zone Pollution (Prevention and Control) Authority: Under Environment Protection Act, 1986. Categories of Industries: Red, Green, Orange, and White based on pollution potential, as defined by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB).
evidenceBullets: The TTZ covers a 10,400 sq km trapezoid-shaped area to prevent pollution around the Taj Mahal.
It encompasses three World Heritage Sites: Taj Mahal, Agra Fort, and Fatehpur Sikri.
The TTZ Pollution Authority is established under the Environment Protection Act, 1986.
Industries in the zone are categorized into Red, Green, Orange, and White by the CPCB based on their pollution potential.

subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
title: Environmental Flow (E-Flow)
focus: Definition and ecological significance of E-Flow in river systems.
rationale: Conceptual topic related to river ecology and hydrology. Good for a single conceptual or definitional question.
sectionExcerpt: Union Jal Shakti Minister led a meeting focused on (E-flow) of Ganga River and its tributaries. About E-Flow: Refers to quantity, timing, and quality of water flow required to sustain freshwater ecosystems and livelihoods that depend on them. Benefits: Maintains ecological integrity of rivers and their estuaries. Extensive interventions in river flow like construction of dams, pollution, and encroachments have severely impacted ecological balance of rivers.
evidenceBullets: E-Flow refers to the quantity, timing, and quality of water flow needed to sustain freshwater ecosystems and dependent livelihoods.
It is crucial for maintaining the ecological integrity of rivers and their estuaries against interventions like dams and encroachments.

subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
title: Air Quality and Pollution Reports
focus: Key findings and publishing bodies of recent environmental reports (SoGA, AQLI, UNCTAD).
rationale: UPSC frequently tests the publishing agencies and key findings of major global environmental indices and reports.
sectionExcerpt: State of Global Air Report (SoGA) 2025 Released by: Health Effects Institute (HEI) and Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME), USA. Mortality: Around 2 million deaths in 2023 linked to air pollution contributing 52% global share. Ozone Pollution: India has third highest exposure. 2025 Annual Update of the Air Quality Life Index (AQLI) Developer: Michael Greenstone and others at Energy Policy Institute at University of Chicago (EPIC). Measures the impact of particulate air pollution on life expectancy. Key Highlights: Air pollution meeting WHO guidelines could extend Indian residents' lives by 3.5 years on average, with Delhi seeing an 8.2-year gain. South Asia continues to be World's most polluted region. Global Trade Update on Mobilising trade to curb Plastic Pollution Released by: UNCTAD.
evidenceBullets: The State of Global Air Report (SoGA) 2025 is released by the Health Effects Institute (HEI) and IHME (USA).
According to SoGA 2025, India has the third highest exposure to Ozone Pollution globally.
The Air Quality Life Index (AQLI) is developed by the Energy Policy Institute at the University of Chicago (EPIC) and measures particulate pollution's impact on life expectancy.
Meeting WHO air quality guidelines could extend Delhi residents' lives by 8.2 years according to the AQLI.
The Global Trade Update on Mobilising trade to curb Plastic Pollution is released by UNCTAD.
